planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in well-drained, average soil in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; star-of-Bethlehem is extremely adaptable and will naturalize readily under deciduous trees and in grassy areas, thriving in the partial shade that filters through open tree canopies. Be aware that it spreads aggressively by offsets and self-seeding and is listed as invasive in several eastern US states — site it thoughtfully.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for April-May bloom; star-of-Bethlehem is a low-growing, cheerful spring bulb producing loose, flat-topped clusters of starry white flowers, each petal boldly striped green on the outside, that open fully only in bright light and close at night and on overcast days — a habit reflected in the old country name 'nap-at-noon.' The narrow, grass-like leaves are dark green with a distinctive pale central stripe, and the plant thrives especially well in the dappled shade beneath deciduous trees, where it receives full spring sun before the canopy closes. It naturalizes freely by offsets and self-seeding, eventually forming large, long-lived colonies that require no attention — a quality that is both a virtue and a caution, as it is considered invasive in parts of the eastern United States and should not be planted where it can escape into natural areas. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep and 6 inches apart in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; bath asparagus tolerates partial shade and does well at the edges of deciduous woodland. In regions where it is edible, the unopened flower spikes may be harvested in May before the flowers open and cooked like asparagus — the remaining stems will still flower normally.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for May-June bloom; bath asparagus (Pyrenean star-of-Bethlehem) is a tall, stately, and distinctly unusual ornithogalum producing elegant 12-24 inch upright spikes of numerous small, pale greenish-yellow, star-shaped flowers that open progressively from the base upward through late spring — the overall effect is airy and quietly striking in the border or at a woodland edge. This species is notably edible: the young, tightly furled flower spikes are traditionally harvested before the flowers open, bundled, and boiled or steamed like asparagus, a practice so ingrained in the English city of Bath that the plant acquired its most familiar common name. Native to Mediterranean scrub and grassy slopes and naturalized across parts of southern England and western Europe, it is well adapted to dry summer dormancy and low-maintenance cultivation. It slowly increases by offsets to form modest clumps and rarely becomes invasive. An unusual dual-purpose plant equally at home in the ornamental border and the edible garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in well-drained to moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; wild hyacinth is the eastern counterpart to the more widely grown C. quamash and is better adapted to the heavier clay soils and summer humidity common across the eastern US. It naturalizes freely and is excellent for planting in large drifts in meadow gardens, native plant borders, or along pond edges.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in well-drained to moderately moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for April-May bloom; wild hyacinth is a graceful native spring bulb widespread across the eastern United States, producing tall, slender spikes of loosely arranged, star-shaped flowers ranging from pale lavender to near-white, the individual blossoms arranged in a loose raceme that opens progressively upward for a long bloom period in mid-spring. It is the eastern ecological counterpart to the western Camassia quamash and shares its history of importance as a food source — Native American peoples across its range harvested, cooked, and dried the nutritious bulbs as a significant caloric staple. In the garden it is one of the most valuable native spring bulbs for naturalizing in meadow plantings, rain gardens, and low areas where the soil stays moderately moist in spring, tolerating both partial shade and heavier clay soils better than most spring bulbs. It combines beautifully with native ferns, Virginia bluebells, and late tulips.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 3 inches apart in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moisture-retentive soil in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; narcissus cyclamineus is one of the earliest-blooming daffodil species and performs best in a sheltered position in partial shade with reliably moist, leafy soil — woodland edges, moist grass, and light shade under deciduous trees are ideal. Avoid hot, dry, or exposed sites.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade in fall for early spring bloom; Narcissus cyclamineus is one of the most distinctive and botanically fascinating of all daffodil species — a diminutive, early-blooming miniature whose most striking feature is its strongly reflexed perianth segments, swept sharply backward like the petals of a cyclamen flower, exposing a disproportionately long, narrow, tubular golden trumpet and giving the flower a dynamic, wind-blown appearance unlike any other narcissus. It is one of the earliest daffodils to bloom, often flowering in late February or early March before many larger daffodils have emerged. This species is the parent of many of the most beloved small daffodil hybrids, including 'Tête-à-tête', 'February Gold', and 'Jack Snipe', and it naturalizes beautifully in moist grass and light woodland shade, increasing steadily over time to form cheerful early-spring colonies. Native to northwest Spain and Portugal, it is less tolerant of dry summer conditions than most daffodils and performs best where the soil retains some moisture year-round.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moisture-retentive soil in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; snake's head fritillary performs best in partial shade in moist, leafy soil similar to a damp meadow or woodland edge — avoid hot, dry, or strongly alkaline sites. Plant in large numbers for the best naturalistic effect; this species self-seeds freely and will increase into generous colonies under favorable conditions.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade in fall for late March-April bloom; snake's head fritillary is one of the most extraordinary and visually arresting spring bulbs in cultivation — a slender, nodding species producing solitary or occasionally paired pendant bells of an intricate, precisely patterned chequered design in rich dusky pink-purple or pure white, each square of the tessellation separated by a deeper or lighter shade in an effect that perfectly mimics the markings of a guinea fowl or the scales of a snake, which gives the plant its common name. Native to damp, traditionally managed flood meadows across Europe, it is tragically endangered in its wild British habitats due to agricultural change, yet it is among the easiest and most rewarding of all spring bulbs in cultivation, naturalizing freely in damp grass, woodland edges, and partial shade where few other bulbs thrive as well. The combination of the hauntingly beautiful flowers, tolerance of shade and moist conditions, cold hardiness to zone 3, and free self-seeding habit makes it one of the finest spring bulbs for naturalistic and woodland plantings.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 6-8 inches deep and 8-10 inches apart in sharply well-drained, alkaline to neutral, gritty or sandy soil in a warm, sheltered, full-sun position in fall, 6-10 weeks before first frost; Persian fritillary is native to rocky, well-drained slopes in Turkey, Iran, and the Middle East and absolutely requires excellent drainage — it will rot in wet or heavy clay soils. In areas with wet winters, plant in raised beds or incorporate grit generously to improve drainage. It may take 2-3 seasons to settle in before flowering freely.

directions: Plant bulbs 6-8 inches deep in sharply well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in fall for April bloom; Persian fritillary is one of the most dramatically beautiful and architecturally impressive of all spring bulbs, producing stout, upright stems 24-36 inches tall clothed in whorls of glaucous blue-grey leaves and terminating in a long, densely flowered raceme of 20-30 or more pendant, narrowly bell-shaped flowers in an extraordinarily rich, deep plum-purple to near-black — a color unmatched in depth and intensity by any other commonly grown spring bulb. The cultivar 'Adiyaman' is particularly dark and free-flowering. Native to rocky limestone slopes, scrub, and dry meadows across Turkey, Iran, and the Levant, it demands the sharp drainage, alkaline soil, and summer baking of its native habitat to perform well, and it is less forgiving of wet, heavy soils than most spring bulbs. In well-suited conditions in a warm, sheltered border with free-draining soil it is a stunning, long-lived perennial that draws attention from every visitor to the spring garden.
